coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots surface chemistry: Evaluation of Photo and Cytotoxic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Prekodravac, Jovana R.
Vasiljević, Bojana R.
Žakula, Jelena
Popović, Maja Č.
Pavlović, Vladimir
Ciasca, Gabriele
Romanò, Sabrina
Todorović Marković, Biljana M.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have exceptional physical-chemical properties that make them suitable for a wide range of uses, such as the photocatalytic removal of organic contaminants from water. Here, we provide a one-step, ecologically friendly, and economical method for creating iron/nitrogen co-doped (Fe/N-CQD) and nitrogen-doped (N-CQD) quantum dot nanomaterials. The characteristics of the developed nanomaterials were investigated with regard to how surface chemistry impacts their capacity for adsorption and photoactivity in a case study of a specific water pollutant reduction. The influence of the two irradiation sources-operating in the UV and visible ranges-on the photoactivity of materials was highlighted. Finally, their eco-friendliness was examined on four different human tumor cell lines.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microwave synthesis
N-doped CQD
Fe/N-doped CQD
Photocatalysis
MB removal efficiency
Citotoxocity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The N-CQD and Fe/N-CQD microwave-assisted synthesis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The research was supported by the Science Fund of the Republic of Serbia , #7741955 , Are photoactive nanoparticles salvation for global infection threat? - PHOTOGUN4MICROBES and by the Ministry of Science, technological development and innovation of the Republic of Serbia grant number 451-03-47/2023-01/200017 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The images of commercially availabele lamp (L source on the left) and home-made cylindrical photoreactor (R source on the right) used for the photocatalytic testing.

The morphological features of synthesized nanomaterials: the AFM images of N-CQD (A) and Fe/N-CQD (D); the statistical analysis of the mean height of N-CQD (B) and Fe/N-CQD (E); the TEM images of the samples N-CQD (C) and Fe/N-CQD (F).
Results for the N-CQD and FeN-CQD samples DLS and Z potential measurements.

The FTIR spectra of N-CQD (A) and Fe/N-CQD (B) samples; The XPS analysis of the N-CQD samples: full-scan spectra (C), the deconvoluted C1s (D) and the deconvoluted N1s (E); the XPS analysis of the Fe/N-CQD samples: full-scan spectra (F), the deconvoluted C1s (G), the deconvoluted N1s (H) and the deconvoluted Fe2p (I).
The UV–vis absorption spectra for N-CQD and Fe/N-CQD samples (A), and PL spectra for N-CQD (B) and Fe/N-CQD (C) samples.
The EDS elemental composition analysis for N-CQD and Fe/N-CQD samples.
Photolysis reaction showing the (A) absorption spectra and (B) removal efficiency percentage of MB dye in different time intervals.
The N-CQD/MB and Fe/N-CQD/MB systems: adsorption/desorption equilibrium estimation (A), photocatalytic reaction L source (B), and photocatalytic reaction R source (C).
Citotoxic activity evaluation of N-CQD and Fe/N-CQD on MIA PaCa-2 and HeLa cells.
Citotoxic activity evaluation of N-CQD and Fe/N-CQD on A375 and PANC-1 cells.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots surface chemistry: Evaluation of Photo and Cytotoxic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fondazione Policlinico Universitario A. Gemelli IRCCS, 00168 Roma, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have exceptional physical-chemical properties that make them suitable for a wide range of uses, such as the photocatalytic removal of organic contaminants from water. Here, we provide a one-step, ecologically friendly, and economical method for creating iron/nitrogen co-doped (Fe/N-CQD) and nitrogen-doped (N-CQD) quantum dot nanomaterials. The characteristics of the developed nanomaterials were investigated with regard to how surface chemistry impacts their capacity for adsorption and photoactivity in a case study of a specific water pollutant reduction. The influence of the two irradiation sources-operating in the UV and visible ranges-on the photoactivity of materials was highlighted. Finally, their eco-friendliness was examined on four different human tumor cell lines.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water, as the main existing source for the ecosystem, is often contaminated by different industrial pollutants of high risk [1–3]. Proper sanitation and clean drinking water would prevent 200 million cases of diarrhea, resulting in 2.1 million fewer fatalities from E. coli-related diarrhea annually. Organic dyes, such as methylene blue (MB), are commonly used in textile or cardboard industries. The worldwide organic dyes market is projected to grow significantly from 2021 to 2027, given the increasing spectrum of the product throughout textiles and paper industries causing the growing incidence of allergy or severe reactions to synthetic dyes [4]. The MB dye, a heterocyclic aromatic chemical, with the molecular formula of C16H18N3SCl, is extremely dangerous employing a strong effect on human beings as much as on the environment [5–7]. Efforts for the removal of organic toxic dyes from natural sources, through adsorption or photocatalysis, over the years grew significant research attention [3,8,9].

Due to photocatalysis's complete degradation of persistent organic compounds in ambient circumstances, it has been widely used as a strategy to deal with the issues of water contaminations. However, the large band gap, frequent photocatalayst aggregation in solution, quick eh+ pair recombination, or activity only under UV irradiation rised as the main disadvantages leading to the low photocatalytic capacity and photoenergy consumption efficiency of the available photocatalysts [10].

Due to ease of functionalization, optical properties and high stability upon irradiation, CQD found application in the treatment of different pollutants [11,12]. Heteroatom doping of CQD nanoparticles can significantly influence their properties and consequently their behaviour upon irradiation. The N doped and Fe/N co-doped CQD are interesting carbon nanoparticles with outstanding photo-luminescent properties. The “bottom-up” synthesis from organic molecules as an efficient route to produce fluorescent CQD from molecules that possess –OH, –COOH, and –CO, functional groups, goes through dehydration and carbonization of carbon source at elevated temperatures [13–15]. The presence of oxygen-containing functional groups provides a good solubility of CQD in water media allowing their application as adsorbents or photocatalyst in organic dyes treatment [9]. Although numerous methods for the N doping and Fe/N co-doping of CQD were reported in the literature, the majority of them are time-consuming, complex and require the usage of large amounts of organic solvents [16–19]. In contrast, microwave-assisted synthesis (MW) revealed remarkable results in a wide range of scientific innovations [20–23]. Microwave heating can dramatically reduce reaction time, increase product yield and enhance product purity by reducing unwanted side reactions compared to conventional heating methods [24]. The MW approach, which is cheap, easy, fast, and environmentally friendly, has demonstrated the ability to successfully synthesize and modify CQD by doping [25–27].

Carbon nanomaterials application in water pollution remediation over the years showed a promissing approach towards the environmental preservation [28,29]. The enhanced electronic structure and charge transfer characteristics of N-doped carbon materials are responsible for their favourable performance in electrocatalytic and photocatalytic reactions. As described in one of our previous publish paper [30], the MW assisted synthesis of N-CQD was easily performed which was a triger to prepare the Fe/N co-doped CQD by a simple one-step MW method in water media.

Guided by the idea that 25% of sunlight is blue light, we explored the time-dependent photoactivity of N-CQD and Fe/N-CQD towards the removal of MB dye from water media by keeping constant reaction conditions. A comparative study of the light source type (UV or visible range) was introduced to explore the synergistic effect of the CQD surface chemistry and irradiation source wavelength on the MB dye removal efficiency. Thought the extended literature survey, we expected the N-CQD to have a good chance of obtaining enhanced catalytic properties by co-doping with Fe. By summarizing the obtained results the Fe/N-CQD showed better photoactivity and removal efficiency for MB dye when compared to N-CQD photocatalyst. For the first time, a home-made photo reactor was built with a specific configuration that resulted in reduced light scattering and better absorption of incident light by the photoactive nanomaterials investigated in this study, when compared to commercially available light source.

In addition, because of their inherent non-cytotoxicity, CQDs are appropriate for a range of biomedical uses. The issue is that the majority of cytotoxicity research conducted to date has been done in the absence of light. However, light is involved in many of the suggested applications for CQDs, including photocatalysis, bioimaging, light-emitting diodes, photovoltaic cells, and more. Even though CQDs are photoactive, it is unknown if they break down in the presence of light and whether this photodegradation might harm human cells. Therefore, the cytotoxicity of both photocatalysts (N-CQDs and Fe/N-CQDs) was investigated on four different human tumor cell lines (HeLa, A375, PANC-1, and MIA PaCa-2) to demonstrate their ecofriendliness. Based on the obtained results, the materials under investigation can be utilized not only as photocatalysts but also as biocidal additives for photoactive membranes, coatings, paints, or epoxy floors, all of which can contribute to a safer and cleaner environment. The objective was to highlight the environmentally friendly feature of doped carbon quantum dots and their broad range of current and future applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The N-CQD and Fe/N-CQD microwave-assisted synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: The N-doped CQD were synthesized by the simple one‒step MW method, starting with 0.1 g ml−1 glucose water solution as a carbon source and ammonium hydroxide (25%) as the nitrogen source, as presented in our previous paper [30]. The Fe/N co-doped CQD synthesis was performed, for the first time, in two steps. The first step involved preparing 0.1 g ml−1 glucose water solution followed by stirring it for 2h at 600 rpm in the presence of urea (5 g) and FeCl3 x 3H2O (5.5 g). In the second step, the prepared solution was submitted to microwave heating (CEM Discover BenchMate) for 1 min at 100 °C and 100 W. Finally, the materials were purified by dialysis (3.5 kDa) and filtering employing various pore size filters (450–10 nm). The detaild information on materials characterization can be found in Supplementary information file.

The MB removal efficiency in the presence of the N-CQD and Fe/N-CQD photocatalysts was examined in batch reaction conditions using two light irradiation sources. The first one was in the form of a commercially available free-standing lamp operating in visible range (470 nm, 10W) which will be further denoted as the L source (Fig. 1 , left), while the second one was a closed cylindrical reactor with six LEDs in a hexagonal orientation, and UV operational range (370 nm, 6W), made in our team and further denoted as the R source (Fig. 1, right).

The reaction of photolysis was performed using 20 ml of MB from the stock solution (0.03 mM), followed by irradiation at different time intervals (10–300 min). To establish the adsorption/desorption equilibrium, the reaction in dark conditions was performed by using 20 ml of MB stock solution, adding a photocatalyst (N-CQD or Fe/N-CQD) in the concentration of 0.5 mg/ml. The reactions were performed at neutral pH while the sampling was performed in time intervals of 10, 30, 60, 90, 120, 180, 240 and 300 min. Finally, a photocatalytic reactions were performed for two types of photocatalytic materials, the N-CQD and Fe/N-CQD, under the same experimental conditions including identical photocatalyst and pollutant concentration, medium pH and reaction time interval. The removal efficiency was monitored by a decrease in the absorption band maximum at 664 nm characteristic for MB dye. The absorbance of MB according to the Beer-Lambert law, is proportional to the concentration, therefore the MB concentration was calculated using the UV–Vis absorbance at λ max = 664 nm.

The removal efficiency of N-CQD and Fe/N-CQD towards MB dye was calculated as:
(5) Removal efficiency (%) = [(C0-Ct)/C0] x 100
where, C
0 is the initial concentration of MB dye, while C
t represents the dye concentration at a defined reaction time.

Four different human tumor cell lines, HeLa (CCL-2) cervical cancer, A375 (CRL-1619) melanoma, PANC-1 (CRL-1469), and MIA PaCa-2 (CRL-1420), both pancreatic cancers, were purchased from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, Manassas, VA, USA) and maintained in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium with 4500 mg/L glucose and l-glutamine (Sigma-Aldrich Chemie GmbH, Steinheim, Germany) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (PAN-Biotech, Aidenbach, Germany), 10000 U/ml penicillin and 10 mg/ml streptomycin solution (Capricorn Scientific, Germany). Cells were cultured under standard conditions at 37 °C in a humidified 5% CO2 atmosphere (Esco Lifesciences Group, Singapore, Singapore).

For cytotoxicity, cells were seeded in flat-bottomed 96-well tissue culture plates (Thermo Fischer Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) at 2000 cells/well density, incubated overnight, and then treated in the dark with N-CQD and Fe/N-CQD, dissolved in sterile water. Applied concentrations were 25, 50, 100, 150, and 200 μM.

Cell viability was determined 24 or 48 h after treatment using a sulforhodamine B (SRB) assay based on the measurement of cellular protein content. The cells were fixed with 10% trichloracetic acid (Sigma-Aldrich Chemie GmbH) for 1 h at 4 °C, washed with water, and stained with 0.4% SRB (Sigma-Aldrich Chemie GmbH) in 1% acetic acid for 15 min at room temperature. The cells were then rinsed with 1% acetic acid to remove the unbound stain. SRB, which stayed bound, was extracted with a 10 mM Tris base. The absorbance was measured at 550 nm with a reference wavelength of 690 nm in a microplate reader (Wallac, VICTOR2 1420 Multilabel counter, PerkinElmer, Turku, Finland). The results were presented as percent of cell viability determined according to the following equitation:
Cell viability (%) = absorbance of sample/absorbance of control × 100

The measurements were made in quadruplicate during each experiment, while each experiment was repeated three times. The results were presented as the Mean ± S.D. (standard deviation). The Student's t-test was used to analyze the significance of the differences between samples, with the significance level set at p < 0.05.

The surface morphology of N-CQD and Fe/N-CQD was examined by AFM and TEM microsopy. The AFM images of N-CQD and Fe/N-CQD samples revealed formation of spherical particles (Fig. 2 A, D). The differences in the particle diameters were confirmed by the AFM miscroscopy analysis, performing the statistical analysis over more than 100 dots per sample. Due to the tendency of the N-CQD to somewhat agglomerate at higher concentrations, samples deposited on Mica discs for AFM characterization were diluted 20 times from their initial concentration. As a result, the N-CQD dots dilution allowed the observation of uniform layers of spherical like shapes at 5 μm2 AFM image (Fig. 2A). On the contrary, the high height/diameter flat objects were observed for N-CQD original concentration, suggesting the N-CQD agglomeration and overlapping during spin coating deposition. The mean height of the N-CQD was measured between 2 and 10 nm, with average of 5 nm (Fig. 2B). The particle size distribution examination revealed the real particles diameters between 15 and 53 nm, with average of 34 nm. The AFM image of Fe/N-CQD samples, on the contrary discovered well separated quasi-spherical structures (Fig. 2D), with mean height in the range of 2–7 nm, and mean value of 4 nm (Fig. 2E). When compared to N-CQD, the Fe/N-CQD statistical size distribution analysis showed a real particle diameters of 15–46 nm and average value below 30 nm.

Comparing obtained results with TEM, images of the N-CQD samples showed the diameter distribution of the produced dots between 9 and 21 nm, with average of 15 nm (Fig. 2C) while fo the Fe/N-CQD dots the diameters were more narroved in size with average estimated to 13 nm (Fig. 2F). Linking the obtained results with DLS measurements, we discovered that when compared to the N-CQD broad particle distribution, the FeN-CQD particle size distribution was higher and more restricted (Fig. 3 ). Understanding the zeta potential is useful since the characteristics of the particle-liquid interface have a significant impact on the physical properties of suspensions and colloids. The measured Z potential values (Fig. 3) for FeN-CQD (−21.4 ± 0.7 mV) and N-CQD (−22.5 ± 1.0 mV) show that FeN-CQD samples have a marginally greater agglomeration potential [31].

The composition of synthesized nanomaterials were investigated using FTIR and XPS analysis (Fig. 4 ). The FTIR spectra of both samples (Fig. 4 A, B) showed the presence of characteristic absorption peak at 3200-3400 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H indicating the presence of hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of synthesized N-CQD and Fe/N-CQD samples. The peak at ∼2930 cm−1 comes from the starching vibrations of the C–H, while the peak at ∼1630 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of aromatics (CO/CC/CN) [32,33]. Peaks located at 1384 and 1373 cm−1 respectively, confirmed the presence of amide C–N bonds owing to the N doping to the CQDs, while C–O stretching vibrations of the carboxylic group were observed at ∼1040 cm−1. The FTIR spectra of N-CQD and Fe/N-CQD are remarkably similar showing that both samples contain hydrophilic groups (–OH, –COOH, –NH2) providing them with a good water solubility and stability. Additionally, the FTIR spectra of Fe/N-CQD samples had small intensity peaks at approximately 600 cm−1 suggesting the presence of Fe–O/Fe–N bonds (Figure B) [34].

The XPS full-scan spectra of N-CQD samples (Fig. 4C) reveald the presence of C (286 eV), O (532 eV) and N (400 eV). The deconvolution of the C1s spectra (Fig. 4D) of the N-CQD sample showed six distinctive peaks positioned at 284.5, 285.2, 286.0, 287.1, 288.1 and 288.9 eV attributed to different carbon bonds. Peaks at 284.5 and 285.2 eV indicate the presence of sp2 and sp3 domains, while other four types of carbon bonds were denoted as C–O, CO, NCO and O–CO, respectively [33]. The deconvolution of the N1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 4E) showed three peaks corresponding to the different states of N. The first one at 399.4 eV suggested the N incorporation in a form of pyridinic–N/NH2, while peaks at 400.3 and 401.7 eV indicated the presence of pyrrolic–N and graphitic–N/NH3 +, respectively [33]. Fig. 4F, represents the full-scan spectra of Fe/N-CQD samples showing the presence of four peaks corresponding to C (285.1 eV), N (399.4 eV), O (531.1 eV) and Fe (710.6 eV). The deconvolution of the C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 4G) showed the presence of four types of carbon bonds at 284.4 (sp2), 285.5 (sp3), 287.4 (NCO) and 289.2 eV (O–CO). The XPS spectrum of N1s (Fig. 4H) showed three peaks as in the case of N-CQD sample, indicating that the N doping was successfully performed and N was introduced into the Fe/N-CQD in the form of pyridinic–N/NH2 (398.5 eV), pyrrolic–N (399.9 eV) and graphitic–N/NH3 + (401.3 eV). Fig. 4I shows that the Fe2p XPS spectrum was deconvoluted into four peaks corresponding to the Fe2+2p3/2 (710.1 eV), Fe3+2p3/2 (715.7 eV), Fe2+2p1/2 (724.3 eV) and Fe3+2p1/2 (729.2 eV). The XPS elemental analysis of the N-CQD and Fe/N-CQD samples are listed in Tables S1 and S2. The obtained results confirmed successful doping of Fe on the surface which coordinate with pyridinic–N/NH2, and pyrrolic–N forming the active sites. The FTIR analysis results were consistent with the XPS results confirming the formation of CQDs surface functional froups.

The optical properties of different doped CQD were investigated in aqueous media using UV–Vis and PL spectroscopy (Fig. 5 ). Both samples showed absorption in the ultraviolet range with a tail extending towards the visible region. The N-CQD sample expressed the three typical absorption peaks Fig. 5A. The first absorption band at 220 nm is characteristic for carbon core and assigned as π-π* transitions of aromatic sp2 domains in N-CQDs, while peaks at 272 and 310 nm corresponds to π–π* and n–π* transitions of the CC (aromatic sp2 domains) and CO/CN (heteroatom-containing bonds) bands in the prepared samples, respectively (Fig. 5A) [33,35,36]. Under the same conditions, the absorption spectrum of Fe-NCQDs (Fig. 5A) showed an increased absorption band extending from 260 to 350 nm, which can be attributed to metal-ligand charge transfer brought on by the electron transition from Fe to N in Fe–N-CQDs. The n-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains has a centre at around 335 nm, confirming the presence of aromatic structure in the Fe/N-CQD sample. From the obtained absorption spectra for N-CQD and Fe/N-CQD samples, using Tauc's plots, the extracted band gap energies were 2.6 eV for N-CQD and 2.3 eV for Fe/N–CQD sample (Fig. S1).

The excitation-dependent PL behavior of N-CQD (Fig. 5B) and Fe/N-CQD (Fig. 5C) samples was further investigated over the wide excitation wavelength range from 350 to 490 nm at a 20 nm interval. The collected data showed how the emission changes for different excitation wavelengths, indicating the red-shift in emission wavelength as the excitation wavelength increases (bathochromic shift). This phenomena is referred as the excitation dependent PL behavior caused by the different surface energy traps on the surface of CQDs. The excitation at 350 nm led to a blue light emission for N-CQD (λ em 445 nm) and Fe/N-CQD (λ em 443 nm), while the excitation at 490 nm resulted in a green color with emission maxima centered at 549 nm for N-CQD and 543 nm for Fe/N-CQD samples. Results showed the tunable emission, from blue to green, for both samples. The highest intensity emission peaks for N-CQD and Fe/N-CQD samples, were obtained under excitation at 390 nm, with maxima centered at 479 and 474 nm, respectively.

The elemental EDS composition analysis of N-CQD and Fe/N-CQD samples (Fig. 6 ) revealed the presence of only C, O, and N peaks, while in addition to these peaks in the Fe/N-CQD samples the presence of Fe peak was noticed. Under the same reaction conditions applied for MW synthesis of different doped CQDs, the higher carbon at% portion was observed in sample N-CQD (66 at%) compared to Fe/N-CQD (64 at%). Additionally, both samples showed almost the same at% of incorporated nitrogen with a small amount of Fe (4 at%) present in the Fe/N-CQD sample. The high O at% in N-CQD (24 at%) and Fe/N-CQD (21 at%) samples specified the high percentage of oxygen surface functional groups which explains a good solubility for both samples in aqueous media. In conclusion, the synthesized N-CQDs and Fe/N-CQDs had no elemental fingerprint of foreign pollutants other than C, O, N, and Fe.

Using a common organic pollutant such as MB dye, the removal efficiency of manufactured N-CQD and Fe/N-CQD materials was examined. Under constant reaction conditions such as pH 7 and room temperature, the MB dye absorption maximum at 664 nm decreased negligibly after 300 min of irradiation (Fig. 7 A). During the first hour of irradiation, the photolysis response indicated limited contaminant degradation of less than 5%, while after 300 min of irradiation, less than 10% MB dye degradation was observed (Fig. 7B). According to this finding, the role of MB's own self-degradation is disregarded.

To assess the system's adsorption/desorption equilibrium, the reaction in the presence of the doped CQD photocatalysts was observed for 60 min under dark conditions (Fig. 8 A). For the set conditions a modest shift towards a higher wavelength for the MB dye absorption maximum was observed, which was linked to the adsorption of the pollutant onto the surface of the photocatalysts (Figs. S2A and B). Sampling in different time intervals for 60 min, showed that the adsorption/desorption equilibrium for both N-CQD/MB and Fe/N-CQD/MB system reaches the platou after 30 min (Fig. 8A).

Therefore, for the photocatalytic testing, the system N-CQD/MB and Fe–N/CQD/MB were kept in dark conditions for 30 min under constant stirring to reach the adsorption/desorption equilibrium, followed by exposure to the L or R irradiation source, operating in visible and UV range, respecticely (Fig. 8 B, C). The N-CQD samples showed slightly better adsorption capacity of 30% when compared to the Fe/N-doped samples (24%) under dark conditions which can be associated with the higher free surface area of N-doped CQD. In photocatalytic testing, when compared to the L source, operating under visible light (Fig. 8B), the R source (Fig. 8C) irradiation under UV light for both systems produced better results.

After only 10 min of N-CQD/MB system irradiation by the L source, the MB adsorption maximum decreased to 68% followed by a consistent modest declining step to 55% after 300 min (Fig. S2C). When compared to the L source, the R source irradiation of the same system produced better results, with the MB absorption maximum decreasing significantly under 300 min to the final 35% (Fig. S2E). When compared to the N-CQD, the Fe/N-CQD photocatalyst, under the L source exposure showed slightly better results (Fig. S2D). Fe/N-CQD influenced the MB adsorption maximum decrease to 63% after 10 min of exposure, while after 300 min under visible light the adsorption droped to 48% (Fig. 8B). When compared to the L source, the results obtained utilizing the R source revealed a more efficient MB absorption maximum decrease (Fig. S2F). As a result, after 90 min, more than 50% of the dye had degraded, while extending the reaction time yielded more progressive outcomes, with the final 84% dye degradation efficiency after 300 min (Fig. 8C).

Obtained results demonstrated obvious differences in the removal efficiency of the MB pollutant under different irradiation sources. In both scenarios (L and R source), the Fe/N-CQD MB dye removal effectiveness outperformed the percentages obtained in the presence of the N-CQD photocatalyst. Therefore, not only the irradiation source type but the photocatalyst surface chemistry affects the pollutant treatment efficiency rate. When comparing the home made reactor type irradiation source (R) to a commercially available UV lamp source (L), the obtained results showed that the R source operating under UV light was more efficient in MB degradation regardless of the photocatalysts surface chemistry. The results clearly displayed that the light source design and wavelength influence the final results. The L source as a visible light free-standing lamp provokes more light scattering than the R source. In comparison, the R design, which consisted of a closed cylindrical tube with a particular LEDs configuration, allowed for more homogenous light dispersion. As a result of the improved system interaction/absorption of the incident light, the degradation rate for the specific MB pollutant was increased using an R source. Parallel to the irradiation source strong effect, the photocatalysts behavior under specific conditions created a considerable variation in photoactivity. Since photocatalysis occurs on the catalyst surface, the performance of the photocatalyst is greatly influenced by the solution pH, the pollutant type and the photocatalysts surface ability to absorb the pollutant [37]. With this in mind, the amount of organic pollutant adsorbed on the surface of the photocatalyst represent a high impact factor on their photocatalytic performance [38]. The nature of the electrostatic interactions between the dye and the photocatalyst surface can explain the difference in adsorption percentages between the two photocatalysts studied. A lower percentage of the adsorbed pollutant was observed on Fe/N-CQD surface (24%) compared to N-CQD (30%) which can be associated with the surface chemistry and coordination in Fe/N-CQD samples. Nevertheless, the Fe/N-CQD showed improved photoactivity in MB degradation compared to the N-CQD. The N doping to CQD promotes the electron injection into the CQD and modify their local electronic structure. Based on the findings of detailed N-CQD analysis, showing the presence of electron-rich species on their surface, predominantly hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups, it is expected that such N-CQD nature will lead to interactions with electron‒deficient species, making them ideal for photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants such as cationic MB dye. In the field of environmental science, the point of zero charge (pzc) determines how easily a substrate is able to adsorb potentially harmful ions. The pzc of N-CQD is 5.88 while for Fe/N-CQD is 6.72, which means that the surface of both CQD is positively charged at pH below these values and negatively charged at pH above these values (Fig. S3). At pH 7 (this is the pH used for the photocatalytic experimental) both CQD have negative charge on the surface, therefore the adsorption of positively charged organic dye (MB) is increased. However, as the experimental results demonstrated, the stronger adsorption was observed in the presence of N-CQD photocatalyst when compared to Fe/N-CQD, probably due to the lower binding of the MB molecules to the Fe/N-CQD photocatalyst due to the repulsion of the cationic molecules and reduction of adsorption of the dye.

In the presence of N‒CQD photocatalysts, the greatest removal efficiency was achieved after 300 min using the R irradiation source (65%), which was better than that of the L source (45%). On the contrary, in the Fe/N-CQD samples the pyridinic‒N and pyrrolic‒N atoms can coordinate with Fe to generate Fe/N active sites. When Fe/N coordination bonds occur, the electron density on the N atoms decreases, which is believed to be due to electron transfer from the N to the Fe atoms. As a result, the N atoms are stabilized whilst the electron density on Fe increases due to the existence of oxygen or nitrogen‒containing functional groups. Consequently, the Fe/N dopants improve CQD electron-accepting and electron-donating properties, as well as their conductivity, which is advantageous to the entire electron‒transfer process and, enhances materials photocatalytic capacity. As a result, after 300 min of irradiation, the R source gave the greatest MB dye removal efficiency percentage in the presence of Fe/N-CQD photocatalyst (84%), while the L source provided 52%.

The viability of the HeLa cells was significantly reduced after the 24h treatment with 100 μM N-CQD (Fig. 9A). In contrast, prolongation of the treatment caused significant stimulation of cell growth with all applied concentrations (108–130%) as presented in Fig. 9B. Under the same experimental conditions, Fe/N-CQD significantly increased cell viability only when the lowest dose was used 24h after treatment (Fig. 9C), with no cytotoxicity after 48h (Fig. 9D). Changes in viability after tretment of MIA PaCa-2 cells with N-CQD was detected after 48h (Fig. 9B), signficant simulation of cell growth for the concentrations of 25–150 μM (p < 0.05 for 25–50 μM; p < 0.01 for 100–150 μM) and inhibition for the highest concentration of 200 μM (p < 0.001). The lowest concentration of Fe/N-CQD significantly stimulate cell viability 24 h (Fig. 9C) after tretment (127%, p < 0.05) while tretmant of 48 h (Fig. 9D) cause decrease in cell viability (p < 0.05 for 100 μM, p < 0.001 for 150–200 μM).

The N-CQD showed similar effect on A375 cells as on HeLa cells. Significant decrease after 24h with 85% for 100 μM and 89% for 150 μM, p < 0.05 (Fig. 10A) and cell growth stimulation after 48h with 130% for 150 μM, p < 0.05 and 127% for 200 μM, p < 0.001 (Fig. 10B).

On the other hand, treatment with Fe/N-CQD revealed no significant cytotoxicity after 24h (Fig. 10C), while prolonged treatment caused a significant decrease in cell viability (Fig. 10D) over the whole range of applied concentrations (p < 0.01). Treatment of PANC-1 cells with N-CQD 24h after treatment showed significant decrease in cell growth (90% for 150 μM, p < 0.05) and increase 48 h after treatment (117% for 100 μM, p < 0.05) (Fig. 10A and B). In contrast, Fe/N-CQD treatment revealed no significant changes in cell growth (Fig. 10C and D).

The results of the presented study demonstrated a green, simple, and quick microwave-assisted approach for producing N doped and Fe/N co-doped CQD from glucose precursors in aquatic media. When compared to literature data, according to the XPS analysis the synthesized Fe/N-CQD had a high atomic percentage of doped Fe (2 at%). The photoactivity of produced materials against MB dye pollutants was investigated using two different types of irradiation sources, indicating that the CQD surface chemistry and irradiation source types had a synergistic influence on the ultimate photocatalytic degradation. In comparison to commercially available sources, the specifically designed reactor for photocatalytic processes with higher utilization of incident light produced better results. Outcomes showed that over 80% of the MB pollutant was removed from the water medium by using a R type source in combination with a Fe/N-CQD photocatalyst after 300 min of light exposure. Therefore, the Fe/N-CQD are an excellent candidate for photocatalytic organic dye removal from aquatic media, whereas the results for N-CQD indicate that it could be employed as a low-cost alternative adsorbent in wastewater treatment for organic dye removal.

Jovana R. Prekodravac: Conceptualization, Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Bojana R. Vasiljević: Methodology, Investigation. Jelena Žakula: Formal analysis. Maja Č. Popović: Formal analysis. Vladimir Pavlović: Data curation. Gabriele Ciasca: Data curation. Sabrina Romanò: Data curation. Biljana M. Todorović Marković: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research was supported by the Science Fund of the Republic of Serbia, #7741955, Are photoactive nanoparticles salvation for global infection threat? - PHOTOGUN4MICROBES and by the Ministry of Science, technological development and innovation of the Republic of Serbia grant number 451-03-47/2023-01/200017.
